claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 4 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let me ask you some questions to determine your eligibility.

**First, I need to know about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is your child? 

- Are they **under 16 years old**?
- Are they **16-19 years old**?
- Or are they **20 or over**?

Please provide their age, and if you have multiple children you'd like to ask about, let me know their ages too.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I've got two children I'm asking about - Alex is 6 and Blake is 4. They're both under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have two children:
- **Alex** (age 6)
- **Blake** (age 4)

Both are under 16, which is great. Let me continue with the assessment for children under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, the next question:

**Are Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Given that they're 6 and 4 years old, I expect they're not in paid work, but I need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not working at all - they're just kids! Alex is in primary school and Blake is still little.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That makes perfect sense! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

This means benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that would be claimed by the child themselves (not by you as their parent). 

Given their ages (6 and 4), I expect they're not receiving these benefits, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they don't receive any benefits in their own names. They're too young for that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Next question:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT in any apprenticeship programs?**

Again, given they're 6 and 4 years old, I expect they're not in apprenticeships, but I need to confirm this to proceed.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not in any apprenticeships - they're way too young for that!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely right! Let me move forward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check your residency status as the claimant.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You'll qualify if ANY of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common situation)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient financial resources
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You're a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're a Crown servant living abroad

Do any of these apply to you? Most people simply normally live in the UK - is that your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex and Blake.

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

You meet this requirement if ANY of the following apply:

1. **You live with the children** (this is the most common situation)
2. You contribute to their upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit would be - through food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents, etc.)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Do you live with Alex and Blake, or do any of the other situations apply?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live with Alex, but Blake doesn't live with me. I do help out with Blake though - I give some money towards their clothes and things like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that! This is an important distinction:

- **Alex (6 years old)**: You live with Alex ✓
- **Blake (4 years old)**: Blake doesn't live with you, but you contribute towards their upkeep

For Blake, I need to understand a bit more about your contributions:

**Do you pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards Blake's care?**

For context, Child Benefit is currently:
- £25.60 per week for the eldest/only child
- £16.95 per week for additional children

Since Blake would be assessed individually, the question is: do your contributions (money for clothes, food, pocket money, housing, presents, etc.) equal or exceed approximately **£25.60 per week** (or about £110 per month)?

This can be paid weekly, monthly, or as lump sum payments.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I give about £20 a week, so a bit less than that amount.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know.

So to summarize:
- **Alex**: You live with Alex, so you meet the responsibility requirement ✓
- **Blake**: You contribute £20 per week, which is less than the Child Benefit amount of £25.60 per week

For Blake, since your contributions are below the Child Benefit threshold, you wouldn't meet the responsibility requirement through financial contributions alone.

Let me continue the assessment for Alex, since you clearly meet the responsibility requirement for them. Then I'll address Blake's situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Next question about Alex:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

- If no one else is claiming, that's straightforward
- If someone else is claiming but you've agreed that you should claim instead, that can work too
- If there's a dispute about who should claim, HMRC would need to decide

What's the situation with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, no one else is claiming for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with Alex's assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check Alex's living situation:

**Which of the following applies to Alex?**

- Is Alex in hospital?
- Is Alex in care (looked after by the local authority)?
- Is Alex fostered?
- Or is Alex living normally (with you at home)?

What's Alex's situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is living normally with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Almost there! One final question about Alex:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the council providing any financial support specifically for Alex's housing or upkeep? Most families support their children themselves without council payments, so this is typically "yes, the council is NOT paying."

What's your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - the council isn't paying anything for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me check the outcome for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news for Alex! Now let me address Blake's situation. 

Since Blake doesn't live with you and your contributions (£20/week) are below the Child Benefit threshold (£25.60/week), you unfortunately **would not meet the responsibility requirement** for Blake. This means you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

**However**, the person who Blake lives with and who has primary responsibility for Blake's care **may be eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Blake, assuming they meet all the other requirements.

Let me now provide you with the complete assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Your Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment Results

---

### ✅ **ALEX (Age 6) - ELIGIBLE**

**Good news!** You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

**Why you're eligible:**
- Alex is under 16 and meets all child-related requirements
- You normally live in the UK
- You live with Alex, establishing your responsibility for them
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- The local council isn't paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance

**Next steps for Alex:**
1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - if you or your partner earn over £60,000, you may need to pay some or all of the benefit back through tax
5. **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

You can apply here: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

---

### ❌ **BLAKE (Age 4) - NOT ELIGIBLE (for you to claim)**

Unfortunately, you are **not eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

**Why you're not eligible:**
- While Blake meets all the child-related criteria (under 16, not working, etc.), you don't meet the **responsibility requirement**
- Blake doesn't live with you
- Your financial contributions of £20 per week fall short of the required threshold of £25.60 per week (the current Child Benefit rate for an eldest/only child)

**Important information about Blake:**
The person Blake lives with (their primary caregiver) **may be eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Blake if they meet all the eligibility requirements. They should consider applying.

---

If you have any questions about applying for Alex or about Blake's situation, please let me know!

reasonings: Alex (age 6) - ELIGIBLE: Alex is under 16 years old, not in paid work, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. You (the claimant) normally live in the UK, meeting residency requirements. You live with Alex, which establishes responsibility for the child. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex is living normally at home with you, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake (age 4) - NOT ELIGIBLE (for you to claim): While Blake is under 16 years old and meets the child-related criteria (not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeship), you do not meet the responsibility requirement for Blake. Blake does not live with you, and your financial contributions of £20 per week fall below the required threshold of £25.60 per week (the Child Benefit rate). Without meeting the responsibility requirement, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake. However, the person Blake lives with may be eligible to claim if they meet all requirements.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (6) and Blake (4). For Alex, the claimant meets all eligibility requirements including UK residency, responsibility (living with the child), and no disqualifying factors, making them eligible to claim Child Benefit. For Blake, although the child meets all child-related criteria, the claimant does not meet the responsibility requirement as Blake doesn't live with them and their financial contributions (£20/week) are below the required threshold (£25.60/week). The claimant can claim Child Benefit for Alex but not for Blake.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child
